title: Carlos Alcaraz Withdraws from French Open Due to Wrist Injury, Opening Door for Rival Sinner
summary:
In a disappointing turn for tennis fans, Carlos Alcaraz, the two-time defending French Open champion, has announced he will miss this year's tournament due to a lingering wrist injury. The 22-year-old world number two sustained the injury to his right wrist during his first-round victory at the Barcelona Open last week. Subsequent tests revealed the severity of the issue, leading him to withdraw from both the Italian Open and the French Open, both played on clay courts.

"After the results of the tests carried out today, we have decided that the most prudent thing to do is to be cautious and not participate in Rome or Roland Garros, while we assess the situation to determine when we can return to the court," Alcaraz posted on Instagram. "This is a difficult time for me, but I am sure we will come out of this stronger."

Alcaraz began 2026 by winning his first Australian Open title, becoming the youngest man to complete the career Grand Slam—winning all four major tournaments at least once. He is particularly dominant on clay, having won titles in Monte Carlo, Rome, and Roland Garros last season. His absence not only costs him the chance to defend his title but also allows his long-time rival, Jannik Sinner, to potentially secure his own career Grand Slam. The French Open is the only major missing from Sinner's collection. Sinner, who recently overtook Alcaraz as world number one, expressed his sympathy: "It is very sad news. Being that young like he is and like I am, we need to look at our bodies first before worse things [happen]. Hopefully he can be back for Wimbledon and we all hope for great battles in the future."
why_it_matters: This story illustrates the physical toll of elite sports and how even young champions like Alcaraz can be sidelined by injuries. It also shows how rivalries drive tennis narratives and how one player's absence can open the door for another to make history, affecting the entire season's drama.

questions.correct_answer: It was the most prudent thing to do

background_read: The French Open, also called Roland Garros, is the premier clay-court tennis tournament and one of the four Grand Slam events. Carlos Alcaraz (a young Spanish tennis star who became the youngest world number one at 19) had won the previous two French Opens and was the defending champion.

His injury occurred at the Barcelona Open, a clay-court tournament. Alcaraz had just completed a career Grand Slam by winning the Australian Open in January 2026, meaning he had won all four majors. He is known for being especially dominant on clay, having won Monte Carlo, Rome, and Roland Garros in 2025.

Jannik Sinner (the Italian world number one and longtime rival of Alcaraz) is now the favorite to win the French Open. Sinner has already won the Australian Open, Wimbledon, and the US Open, so the French Open is the only major missing from his own career Grand Slam. Sinner expressed sympathy for Alcaraz and hopes for future battles at Wimbledon.

This situation highlights how injuries can reshape tournament outcomes and how professional tennis players must balance the drive to compete with long-term health. The rivalry between Alcaraz and Sinner is one of the most exciting in recent tennis history.

perspectives.description: Alcaraz made a wise long-term decision for his health, preserving his career. Jannik Sinner gets a golden chance to complete his own career Grand Slam, adding excitement to the tournament.
Fans lose the defending champion and a potential classic final. The tournament's competitiveness may diminish without Alcaraz, and the injury raises concerns about the toll of the clay season.

Injuries are an unavoidable part of sports; they test the depth of the player field. The French Open will still feature many top players, and the Alcaraz-Sinner rivalry remains one of the most compelling in tennis.
If Alcaraz recovers fully, he could return for Wimbledon and rekindle the rivalry. His injury may prompt discussions about scheduling reforms to prevent such issues in young stars.
